decision: Skill wrapper as primary integration for context-hub, not MCP server in plugin.json
rationale: Adding npm/Node.js MCP server to plugin.json creates hard runtime dependency for all users in Python-primary ecosystem. Skill is opt-in, zero cost when not loaded, follows existing pattern.

decision: Disable context-hub telemetry by default in all integration documentation and examples
rationale: Praxion philosophy prioritizes intentional context. Surprise analytics collection violates pragmatism principle. Users can re-enable explicitly.
alternatives: Document opt-out but do not enforce in examples

decision: Defer registry publishing of Praxion skills to context-hub registry
rationale: Praxion skills reference internal components (agents, commands, rules) that external consumers cannot resolve. Publishing would create broken experiences.

decision: Use canonical Node.js context-hub implementation, not Rust reimplementation
rationale: Canonical implementation controls CDN, receives content PRs, has Andrew Ng team behind it. Rust 19x speed advantage irrelevant for agent-invoked CLI calls.

decision: Chronograph as OTel relay: hooks POST to chronograph, which creates OTel spans and exports to Phoenix via OTLP HTTP. No direct hook-to-Phoenix export.
rationale: Chronograph already has full agent state for correct span hierarchy. Hook scripts stay stdlib-only (<100ms). No OTel Collector infrastructure needed.
alternatives: Direct export from hooks (needs OTel SDK in hooks)

decision: Dedicated Phoenix venv at ~/.phoenix/venv/ separate from chronograph's environment.
rationale: Phoenix has heavy deps (numpy, pandas, sqlalchemy) that could conflict. Isolation prevents upgrade cascades.

decision: CHAIN span kind for session root, AGENT for pipeline agents in OpenInference convention.
rationale: CHAIN is semantically correct for orchestration/linking. AGENT reserved for reasoning blocks. Phoenix renders both with appropriate UI treatment.
alternatives: AGENT for root (conflates orchestration with reasoning)
Custom SESSION kind (not in OpenInference spec)

decision: Dual storage: EventStore (in-memory) for real-time MCP queries + OTel/Phoenix for persistent traces. Both maintained in parallel.
rationale: MCP tools need fast in-memory reads without Phoenix dependency. Phoenix provides persistence and UI. Independent failure domains.
alternatives: OTel only (MCP queries need network to Phoenix)

decision: Dashboard redirect to Phoenix UI instead of parallel operation or immediate removal.
rationale: Phoenix provides strictly superior trace UI. Redirect guides existing users. Removes dead dashboard code and jinja2/sse-starlette dependencies.

decision: Single send_event.py for all hook events (SessionStart, Stop, SubagentStart/Stop, PostToolUse, PostToolUseFailure) rather than multiple scripts.
rationale: All hooks share HTTP POST logic and port config. Single script avoids duplication. Adding branches is ~10 lines per event type.
